namespace functions {

// Count to, for testing progress and cancellation
type count = (_: {
// the integer to count to
to: number,
}) => any;

// Delegate to a subagent to perform relevent tasks and summarize findings.
type delegate = (_: {
// which subagent profile to use, options: assistant, web-researcher, command-runner, file-finder, test-finder
agent_type?: string,
// describe the task for the agent to perform
description: string,
// LangChain's max messages for the subagent before stopping. Each subagent tool call produces 2 messages (input + output). Leave unset unless the task needs more or fewer than the default (50).
recursion_limit?: number,
}) => any;

// Run a process on this darwin machine
type run_process = (_: {
// Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.
argv?: string[],
// Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.
command_line?: string,
// Optional to set working directory
cwd?: string,
// Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms
timeout_ms?: number,
// Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.
stdin_text?: string,
}) => any;

// Fetch a URL and extract HTML contents as markdown
type fetch = (_: {
// Start at this character offset, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required
start_index?: number, // default: 0,
// Maximum number of characters to return
max_chars?: number, // default: 50000,
// Do not convert HTML to markdown
raw?: boolean, // default: false,
url: string,
}) => any;

// Execute Lua code in the current Neovim process (that hosts your agent). For example, call vim.cmd()
type run_in_neovim = (_: {
// Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.
lua: string,
}) => any;

import iterm2
import asyncio
import traceback

from f9command import on_f9

from og_ask import ask_openai

async def main(connection: iterm2.Connection):

    async def keystroke_handler(keystroke: iterm2.Keystroke):
        # *** by registering a keystroke monitor in an iterm2 daemon, and reacting to just my keycombos... this script is warmed up and can immediately dispatch!!! shaves 1-2 seconds off of running a python script on keycombo instead... this is awesome

        control = iterm2.Modifier.CONTROL in keystroke.modifiers
        shift = iterm2.Modifier.SHIFT in keystroke.modifiers
        command = iterm2.Modifier.COMMAND in keystroke.modifiers
        option = iterm2.Modifier.OPTION in keystroke.modifiers

        # print_keystroke(keystroke)

        # FYI keystroke monitor only works if:
        #   - Script Console is not focused
        #   - iTerm2 window is focused
        #   - that means, if no iTerm2 windows are open, then keystroke monitor won't work
        #   WORKAROUND: use keyboard maestro to remap key combos and run an exteral script that uses iterm's python API... s/b fine
        #      one benefit: I don't have to reload the builtin wes.py script!

        # notes:
        #   AFAICT iterm won't let me remap builtin keys using a keystroke monitor alone
        #   there is an "Ignore" in Settings => Keys but then my monitor doesn't receive it either
        #     there is a "Script function" section but I cannot find any docs about what it does
        #     otherwise, pretty much can only remap to another existing command (limited subset too) and that sucks
        #     they should have a python handler alterantive or "Do Nothing" vs "Ignore" which squelches it
        #   so, in KMaestro I remap Cmd+N => Cmd+Shift+Control+N
        #     also Cmd+T => Cmd+Shift+Control+T
        # *** New Window helpers
        # FYI also had to remap Cmd+N => Cmd+Shift+Control+N in Keyboard Maestro
        n = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_N
        if n and control and shift and command:
            await wes_new_window(connection, force_local=False)
            return
        if n and command and control:
            await wes_new_window(connection, force_local=True)
            return

        # *** New Tab helpers
        # FYI also had to remap Cmd+T => Cmd+Shift+Control+T in Keyboard Maestro
        t = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_T
        if t and control and shift and command:
            await wes_new_tab(connection, force_local=False)
            return
        # FYI also had to remap Cmd+Shift+T in KM => Cmd+Ctrl+T
        #    cannot remap to same keys, wouldn't work :)
        if t and command and control:
            await wes_new_tab(connection, force_local=True)
            return

        # *** Split panes helpers (ssh support)

        # FYI KM => remaps Cmd+D (split vert) => Cmd+Shift+Control+D

        #     avoid:
        #       (cmd+ctrl+d == define universally)
        #       (ctrl+alt+d == toggle dock)
        d = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_D
        if d and control and shift and command:
            await wes_split_pane(connection, split_vert=True)
            return
        if d and control and command and option:
            await wes_split_pane(connection, split_vert=False)
            return

        b = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_B
        if b and control and shift and command:
            await ask_openai(connection)
            return

        # keymap doesn't matter, just update streamdeck button if change this:
        d = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_X
        if d and control and shift and command:
            await close_other_tabs(connection)
            return

        # keymap doesn't matter, just update streamdeck button if change this:
        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_Y
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await new_tab_then_close_others(connection)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_F
        if e and control and shift and command:
            # TODO merge with ANSI_B? should I just use this instead of ANSI_B approach?
            await copy_screen_to_clipboard(connection, history=False)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_H
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await copy_screen_to_clipboard(connection, history=True)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.F9
        if e:
            await on_f9(connection)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_MINUS
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await smaller_font_wes_stops(connection)
            return
        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_EQUAL
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await bigger_font_wes_stops(connection)
            return

    async def keystroke_monitor(connection):
        async with iterm2.KeystrokeMonitor(connection) as mon:
            while True:
                # unhandled exceptions take down the monitor so don't let that happen anymore!
                try:
                    keystroke = await mon.async_get()
                    await keystroke_handler(keystroke)
                except Exception as e:
                    log(f"unhandled exception in keystroke_monitor: {e}")
                    # dump stack trace
                    traceback.print_exc()

    asyncio.create_task(keystroke_monitor(connection))
    asyncio.create_task(semantic_daemon(connection))

iterm2.run_forever(main)

`get_current_session*()` (and the helpers it uses) walk the object graph

```python
app = await iterm2.async_get_app(connection)
window = app.current_window
tab    = window.current_tab
session = tab.current_session
```

That works fine when the *focus* is already on the pane you want.  
When you invoke a hot‑key that creates a new tab/pane (or when you have several tabs open) the focus‑change happens **after** the keystroke is delivered to the daemon.  
So the code runs a split‑/new‑tab command, but the `app.current_window / current_tab / current_session` you read is still the old one (the “initial” session).  
The result is exactly the symptom you described: the script sometimes uses the session of the first tab instead of the pane that is actually focused.

**Where the logic is off**

All of the helpers in `common.py` are fine – they correctly return the *currently focused* session **at the moment they are called**.  
The problem is *when* they are called.

Examples in `split.py`:

```python
# wes_new_window
session = await get_current_session_throw_if_none(connection)   # ← this is the old session
...
new_window = await iterm2.Window.async_create(...)

# wes_split_pane
session = await get_current_session_throw_if_none(connection)   # ← old session
new_session = await session.async_split_pane(...)
```

Because the key‑press that triggered the command is still being processed, the focus has not yet moved to the newly created pane/tab, so `session` points at the wrong pane.

**Fix – fetch the *focused* session **after** the UI change has taken effect**

The simplest fix is to defer the “get the current session” call until *after* the new tab/pane/window is created (or after a tiny pause).  
Below is a tiny helper that guarantees you get the session that actually has the input focus at the moment you call it:

```python
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# common.py  – add at the end of the file
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def get_focused_session(connection: iterm2.Connection) -> iterm2.Session:
    """
    Return the session that currently has the keyboard focus.
    This should be called *after* any UI operation that may change focus
    (e.g. after creating a new tab/pane/window) so you get the right
    session.
    """
    app = await iterm2.async_get_app(connection)
    if app is None:
        raise Exception("Failed to get iTerm2 app object")
    window = app.current_window
    if window is None:
        raise Exception("No current window")
    tab = window.current_tab
    if tab is None:
        raise Exception("No current tab")
    session = tab.current_session
    if session is None:
        raise Exception("No current session")
    return session
```

**Use it in the three entry points**

```python
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# split.py  – replace the “old” session look‑ups
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# 1️⃣  New window
async def wes_new_window(connection: iterm2.Connection, force_local=False):
    prior_window = await get_current_window(connection)
    if prior_window is None:
        log("UNEXPECTED NO PRIOR WINDOW, opening new window")
        await iterm2.Window.async_create(connection)
        return

    # Get the *source* session **before** we create the new window
    src_session = await get_current_session_throw_if_none(connection)
    new_profile, is_ssh = await prepare_new_profile(src_session, force_local)

    # Create the window first
    new_window = await iterm2.Window.async_create(connection,
                                                  profile_customizations=new_profile)
    if new_window is None:
        raise Exception("UNEXPECTED NO WINDOW CREATED")

    # Now ask iTerm2 which session actually got focus in the new window
    new_session = await get_focused_session(connection)

    # …rest of the logic (cd, etc.) stays the same
    ...

# 2️⃣  New tab
async def wes_new_tab(connection, force_local=False):
    prior_window = await get_current_window_throw_if_none(connection)
    src_session   = await get_current_session_throw_if_none(connection)
    new_profile, is_ssh = await prepare_new_profile(src_session, force_local)

    # Create the tab
    new_tab = await prior_window.async_create_tab(profile_customizations=new_profile)
    if new_tab is None:
        raise Exception("UNEXPECTED NO TAB CREATED")

    # Get the session that now has focus (the newly created tab)
    new_session = await get_focused_session(connection)

    if not (force_local or is_ssh):
        path = await get_path(src_session)
        await new_session.async_send_text(f"cd {path}; clear\\n")
    ...

# 3️⃣  Split pane
async def wes_split_pane(connection: iterm2.Connection,
                         split_vert: bool = False,
                         force_local=False):
    src_session = await get_current_session_throw_if_none(connection)
    new_profile, is_ssh = await prepare_new_profile(src_session, force_local)

    path = await get_path(src_session)

    # Split – this returns the *new* session object directly,
    # but the focus may still be on the old pane for a split.
    new_session = await src_session.async_split_pane(vertical=split_vert,
                                                    profile_customizations=new_profile)
    if new_session is None:
        raise Exception("UNEXPECTED NO SESSION CREATED")

    # Ensure the new pane really has focus before we act on it
    # (a tiny pause is enough for iTerm2 to update its focus state)
    await asyncio.sleep(0.05)          # 50 ms – adjust if needed
    focused = await get_focused_session(connection)

    # `focused` should be the same as `new_session`, but using it guarantees we
    # are sending commands to the pane the user sees.
    if not (force_local or is_ssh):
        await focused.async_send_text(f"cd {path}; clear\\n")
```

**Why this solves the bug**

* The helper always walks the *current* UI state, so after the UI has settled it returns the right pane.
* In `wes_split_pane` we add a tiny `await asyncio.sleep(0.05)` – the split operation is asynchronous; iTerm2 needs a few milliseconds to promote the new pane to the focus stack. The pause is negligible and guarantees the focus‑query sees the new pane.
* In `wes_new_window`/`wes_new_tab` we no longer rely on `get_current_session_for_window_throw_if_none`, which can give you the session of the *old* tab if the new window/tab hasn’t been activated yet.

        if d and control and command and option:

        b = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_B
        if b and control and shift and command:
            await ask_openai(connection)
            return

        # keymap doesn't matter, just update streamdeck button if change this:
        d = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_X
        if d and control and shift and command:
            await close_other_tabs(connection)
            return

        # keymap doesn't matter, just update streamdeck button if change this:
        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_Y
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await new_tab_then_close_others(connection)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_F
        if e and control and shift and command:
            # TODO merge with ANSI_B? should I just use this instead of ANSI_B approach?
            await copy_screen_to_clipboard(connection, history=False)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_H
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await copy_screen_to_clipboard(connection, history=True)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.F9
        if e:
            await on_f9(connection)
            return

        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_MINUS
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await smaller_font_wes_stops(connection)
            return
        e = keystroke.keycode == iterm2.Keycode.ANSI_EQUAL
        if e and control and shift and command:
            await bigger_font_wes_stops(connection)
            return

    async def keystroke_monitor(connection):
        async with iterm2.KeystrokeMonitor(connection) as mon:
            while True:
                # unhandled exceptions take down the monitor so don't let that happen anymore!
                try:
                    keystroke = await mon.async_get()
                    await keystroke_handler(keystroke)
                except Exception as e:
                    log(f"unhandled exception in keystroke_monitor: {e}")
                    # dump stack trace
                    traceback.print_exc()

    asyncio.create_task(semantic_daemon(connection))

async def close_other_tabs(connection):
    current_tab = await get_current_tab_throw_if_none(connection)

    window = await get_current_window_throw_if_none(connection)

    new_tab = await window.async_create_tab()

async def prepare_new_profile(session: iterm2.Session, force_local_fish: bool) -> tuple[iterm2.LocalWriteOnlyProfile, bool]:
    tab = session.tab
    tab_id = tab.tab_id if tab is not None else "missing tab"

    window_id = window.window_id if window is not None else "missing window"
    print(f"session_id={session.session_id}, tab_id: {tab_id}, window: {window_id}")

    new_profile.set_initial_directory_mode(iterm2.InitialWorkingDirectory.INITIAL_WORKING_DIRECTORY_RECYCLE)

    jobName = await session.async_get_variable("jobName")
    commandLine = await session.async_get_variable("commandLine")
    was_sshed = jobName == "ssh"

    is_ssh = was_sshed and not force_local_fish
    print(f"{force_local_fish=}, {is_ssh=}")

            new_profile.set_use_custom_command("No")

        was_bash = jobName == "bash"

    return new_profile, is_ssh
